paragraphs.text: “In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth” (Gen 1:1) — that is, the substance of heaven and the substance of earth. No one should think that the six-day creation is an allegory. Neither is it permissible to say that what is described as created in the course of six days was in fact created in a single instant, nor that the descriptions are mere names — either signifying nothing or signifying something else. On the contrary, one must know that just as the heaven and earth created in the beginning are truly heaven and earth and not something else hidden under the names “heaven” and “earth,” so also all that is said about everything else which was created and brought to order after the heaven and earth: the names are not empty, but the very substance of the created natures corresponds to the force of these names.

“In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.” The primal work of creation is bounded by this, for nothing else was created together with the heaven and the earth. Even the natures created on that same day had not yet been brought forth; if God had created them together with the heaven and the earth, Moses would have said so. He does not say so, lest one think that the names of natures are older than their existence. From this it becomes clear that heaven and earth were created out of nothing, because as yet no waters existed, nor air, nor had fire, light, or darkness yet come into being. These were produced later than heaven and earth, and so they are creatures, for they came forth after heaven and earth; and they are not eternal, for they did not exist before heaven and earth.

After this, Moses speaks not of what is above the firmament, but of what is between the firmament and the earth, as though in some bosom. He did not write to us about the spirits, nor does he say on what day they were created. Concerning the earth, he writes that it was “without form and void” (Gen 1:2) — that is, it bore nothing upon itself and was empty. He said this wishing to show that the emptiness arose before the natures. Yet I do not say that the emptiness is something actually existing; I only mean to show that at that time the earth alone existed, and besides it nothing else existed. Having spoken of the creation of heaven and earth and pointed out the emptiness (since the time is older than the natures created in time), Moses turns to describing the natures themselves and says, “and darkness was upon the face of the deep” (Gen 1:2). This shows that the abyss of waters was created at the same time. But how was it created on the day on which it was created? Although it was created on that day and at that time, still Moses did not write here how it was created; we must therefore accept that the abyss appeared at the time written, and we must wait for Moses himself to explain how it was created. Some take the darkness “upon the face of the deep” to be the shadow of heaven. If the firmament had been created on the first day, their opinion could hold. And if the upper heavens were like the firmament, deep darkness would have lain between heaven and heaven, because God had not yet created and set up the light to scatter the darkness there with its rays. But if the heavenly region is luminous, as Ezekiel, Paul, and Stephen testify, and the heavens by their light dispel darkness, how then did they spread darkness over the deep?

If everything created (whether it is written about or not) was created in six days, then the clouds appeared on the first day. Fire arose together with the air, though it is not written about; in the same way, the clouds were created together with the deep, though it is not written that they were created together with the deep — just as it is not written that fire was created together with the air. For it was needful that all things be created in six days. The origin of clouds is known to us, and so we must hold that the clouds were created together with the deep, since clouds are always born from the deep. “And Elijah saw a cloud rising out of the sea” (1 Kgs 18:44), and Solomon says, “By his knowledge the depths are broken up, and the clouds drop down the dew” (Prov 3:20). That the clouds were created precisely at this time, namely in the first night, is confirmed for us not only by the very nature of clouds but also by their action — for we hold that the first night was produced by them. Just as the clouds spread over Egypt for three nights and three days and produced night, so the clouds spread over the whole world in the first night and the first day of creation. If the clouds were transparent, then the first day had some illumination, because the radiance of the upper heavens was sufficient to substitute for the light that was created afterward on the same first day.

When the night and the day had passed, in the evening of the second, the firmament was created, and from that time its shadow produced the nights that followed. Thus, in the evening before the first night, God created the heaven and the earth, and with them the deep and the clouds; and these, spreading over everything, produced the dark night. After this shadow had covered everything for twelve hours, the light was created, and it scattered the darkness spread over the waters. Having said of the darkness that it was spread “upon the face of the deep,” Moses continues, “and the Spirit of God was moving over the waters” (Gen 1:2). The Spirit of God is the Holy Spirit of God the Father, proceeding from Him not temporally, equal to the Father and to His Only-begotten Son in essence and creative power. This Spirit, distinguished particularly and independently from the Father, is in the Divine Scriptures called the Spirit of God and the Holy Spirit. Of Him it is said that “He moved over the waters, to impart a fruitful power to the waters, to the earth, and to the air, so that they might become fruitful, conceive within themselves, and bring forth plants, animals, and birds.” It befitted the Holy Spirit to move over the waters in order to attest that He is equal in creative power with the Father and the Son. For the Father spoke, the Son created; the Spirit too had to contribute His own work. And He showed this by “moving,” clearly demonstrating thereby that everything was brought into being and accomplished by the Trinity. One must also know that Scripture, when it tells of the creative power of the Godhead, does not present to us some other spirit which, as a created and produced thing, moved with God over the waters — but it speaks of the Holy Spirit. He warmed the waters, made them fruitful, and rendered them capable of bringing forth, like a bird when she sits with outstretched wings on her eggs, and during that outspreading warms them with her warmth and produces fruitfulness in them. This same Holy Spirit then prefigured for us the image of Holy Baptism, when by His “moving” over the waters He brought forth the children of God.

Having spoken of the creation of the heaven, the earth, the darkness, the deep, and the waters at the beginning of the first night, Moses turns to recount the creation of light in the morning of the first day. So, when the twelve hours of night had passed, light was created amid the clouds and waters, and it scattered the shadow of the clouds that hung over the waters and produced the darkness. Then began the first month of Nisan, in which the days and nights have an equal number of hours. The light had to remain for twelve hours, so that the day might contain the same number of hours as the measure and length of time the darkness had remained. For although both the light and the clouds were created in the blink of an eye, yet both the day and the night of the first day lasted twelve hours each.

The light that appeared upon the earth was like a luminous cloud, or like the rising sun, or like the pillar that gave light to the Hebrew people in the wilderness. In any case it is certain only that the light could not have scattered the darkness that enveloped everything had it not spread its substance everywhere or its rays, like the rising sun. The primordial light was poured forth everywhere, not confined to one particular place; it dispersed the darkness everywhere without movement; all its movement consisted in appearing and disappearing. With its sudden disappearance came the dominion of night, and with its appearance the dominion of night ceased. So that the light would not be turned to nothing, as that which had come out of nothing, God said of it expressly, “it is good” (Gen 1:4). Thereby He testified that “very good” are all the creatures that came before the light, of which it was not said that they were good. For though God did not say it of them at the very moment of creating them out of nothing, He confirmed it of them later when He had formed all creatures. To all that was created in the six days apply the words spoken at the end of the sixth day: “And God saw everything that he had made: and behold, it was very good” (Gen 1:31).

That primordial light, called good when it was created, by its rising produced (formed) three days. It, as is said, contributed to the conception and bringing forth of everything that the earth was to produce on the third day; the sun, established upon the firmament, was to bring to ripeness what had already come forth with the help of the primordial light. They say that out of that light dispersed everywhere and out of the fire created on the first day, the sun was made which is upon the firmament, and that the moon and the stars are from that same primordial light. Therefore just as the sun, ruling the days, ought to illumine the earth and also bring its products to ripeness, so the moon, ruling the nights, not only tempers the heat of the night with its light, but also helps the earth to bring forth the fruits and produce proper to its primary nature. And Moses in his blessings says, “from the fruit which the moon brings forth” (Deut 33:14). Concerning light, it is noted that, among other reasons, it was created on the first day also for the earth’s produce. But the earth, with the help of the light, produced all that was accomplished on the third day, though the light was in its primordial state; nevertheless, all the earth’s fruits, by the help of the moon as by the help of the light, received their beginning; with the help of the sun they came to ripeness.

So the earth produced everything from itself with the help of light and waters. Although God could have brought forth everything from the earth without them, yet such was His will, that thereby He might show that all that was created upon the earth was created for the benefit of man and for his service.

The waters that covered the earth on the first day were not salty. Although the deep of waters stood over the earth, the seas had not yet appeared. The waters became salty in the seas; before they were gathered into seas, they were not salty. When the waters were spread over the face of the earth for its watering, they were sweet. But when they were gathered on the third day into seas, they became salty, lest by being collected into one place they should fall into corruption, and lest, receiving the rivers that flow into them, they should overflow. The waters of the rivers flowing into the sea served it as sufficient nourishment: so that the sea should not dry up from the sun’s heat, rivers flow into it; and so that the sea should not expand, go beyond its bounds, and flood the earth, in receiving the rivers’ waters, those waters are absorbed by the saltness of the sea.

If we suppose that with the creation of the waters the seas too were created and were covered (filled) with waters, and that the waters of the seas were bitter, then we must also say that the waters above the seas were not bitter. For although the seas created then were covered with waters at the time of the Flood, they could not impart their bitterness to the sweet waters of the Flood which stood over the seas. And if the seas could have made the Flood’s waters bitter, how would olives and all other earthly produce have been preserved in them? Or how would Noah and those with him have drunk them during the Flood? Noah was commanded to bring into the ark food for himself and all who were with him, because there would be nowhere to obtain food; but he was not commanded to bring water, because those in the ark could drink the water that surrounded the ark on every side. Thus, just as the Flood’s waters were not salty although they covered the seas, so the waters gathered on the third day were not bitter, though the waters of the seas beneath them would have been bitter already.

But since the gathering of the waters did not happen before God said, “Let the waters be gathered, and let the dry land appear” (Gen 1:9), there were certainly no seas before God “called the gathering of the waters seas” (Gen 1:10). Therefore the seas, having received a name and occupied their receptacle, were changed and took on the saltness which they did not have before entering their receptacle. And the very receptacle of the seas became sunk precisely at the time when it was said, “Let the waters be gathered into one place” — that is, either the bottom of the seas was lowered beneath the rest of the earth and together with the waters that were over it received the waters that had been over all the earth, or the waters swallowed one another so that there might be room for them, or the bed of the sea split, and a great hollow was formed, so that in the blink of an eye the waters rushed down the slope of the bottom. Although the waters were gathered together by God’s command, yet at the very creation of the earth a door was opened to them, that they might be gathered into one receptacle.

Just as at the first and second gatherings of the waters there was no enclosed place from which they could not go out, so afterward they go out by various streams and springs and gather into their seas by those paths and ways that were laid for them from the first day.

The upper waters too, divided on the second day from the rest of the waters by the firmament stretched between them, were as sweet as the lower waters. They are not such as the waters that became salty in the seas on the third day, but such as the ones separated from them on the second day. They are not salty because they are not subject to corruption. They are not on the earth, from which they might rot, for upon the earth the air does not act so as to make the waters bring forth and produce creeping things. For those waters there is no need of rivers flowing into them; they cannot dry up, because there is no sun to dry them with its heat; those waters abide there as a dew of blessings, and are kept for the pouring out of wrath.

Neither is it possible to suppose that the waters above the firmament are in motion, for what has been brought to order does not whirl without order, and what is, is not set in motion by what is not. What is created in something else, at the moment of its creation, receives for itself everything: movement, ascent, and descent, all within that in which it was created. But the upper waters are not surrounded by anything, and so they cannot flow downward or whirl, for there is nothing along which they could flow down or whirl.

So, by Scripture’s testimony, heaven, earth, fire, air, and water were created out of nothing, while the light, created on the first day, and all else that was created after it, were created from what already existed. For when Moses speaks of what was created from nothing, he uses the word “created” — “God created the heaven and the earth.” And although it is not written of fire, water, and air that they were created, neither is it said that they were produced from what was already. Therefore they too were created from nothing, just as heaven and earth were created from nothing. But when God begins to create from what already exists, then Scripture uses an expression like this: “And God said: Let there be light” (Gen 1:3), and so on. And if it is said, “And God created great whales” (Gen 1:21), this is preceded by, “Let the waters bring forth abundantly the moving creature that hath life” (Gen 1:20). Therefore only the five kinds of creatures named above were created from nothing; everything else was made from what had already been created from nothing.

Fire too was created on the first day, although this is not written, because it is contained in another. As something that does not exist by itself and for itself, it was created together with that in which it is contained. As something existing not for itself, it could not have come before that which forms the ultimate cause of its being. Fire is in the earth, to which nature itself bears witness; but Scripture does not declare that fire was created together with the earth, saying simply, “In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.” Therefore, although now fire is not only in earth, but in waters, in the wind, and in clouds, yet earth and waters are commanded at all times to bring it forth from their depths.

Darkness too is not something eternal; it is not even a creature, for darkness, as Scripture shows, is a shadow. It was not created before heaven nor after the clouds, but — together with the clouds, and was produced by them. Its existence depends on another, for it has no substance of its own, and when that on which it depends ceases to be, then with it and like it darkness too ceases to be. But what ceases together with another that has ceased is close to non-existence, for another is the cause of its being. Therefore the darkness that was with the clouds and the firmament, and was no more with the primordial light and with the sun — could it have been self-existent, when one thing by spreading produced it, and another by appearing scattered it? But if one thing produces darkness and gives it being, and another reduces it to nothing, can it be deemed eternal? For behold, the clouds and the firmament, which were created in the beginning, produced darkness; and the light, created on the first day, scattered it. If one creature produced it and another scattered it — the one constantly, together with itself and at the same hour, bringing it into appearance, while the other reduces it to nothing precisely when it is reduced to nothing — it must be concluded that one gives a beginning to its being and the other ends its being. So, if creatures (created things) give darkness being and end it, it follows that darkness is a product of creatures (for it is the shadow of the firmament), and that darkness ceases to be at another creature (for it disappears at the sun). And this darkness, which is wholly enslaved to creatures, certain teachers regard as hostile to creatures! They claim that it, having no substance of its own, is eternal and self-existent!

Moses, having spoken of what was created on the first day, proceeds to describe the creation on the following day, and says: “And God said: Let there be a firmament in the midst of the waters, and let it divide the waters from the waters which were under the firmament and the waters which were above the firmament” (Gen 1:6–7). The firmament, set between waters and waters, had the same extension as the waters spread over the surface of the earth. Since over the firmament there are waters such as the waters that are over the earth, and under the firmament earth, waters, and fire, the firmament is enclosed in this, as a child in its mother’s womb.

Some, supposing the firmament to be in the middle of all creation, regard it as the womb of the universe. But if the firmament had been created as the middle of the universe, then the light, the darkness, and the air, which were over it when God was forming it, would have remained over the firmament. If the firmament was created at night, then together with the waters that remained there, darkness and air too would have remained over the firmament. If it was created by day, then together with the waters, light and air would have remained there. And if they remained there, then those that are here are already others. The question then is: when were they created? But if they did not remain there, how did the natures that were over the firmament at the time of its creation change place and end up beneath the firmament?

The firmament was created in the evening of the second night, just as the heaven was created in the evening of the first night. With the arising of the firmament the shadow of the clouds vanished, the clouds that had served instead of a firmament throughout the night and day. Since the firmament was created between light and darkness, the darkness took up its place above the firmament as soon as, with the clouds removed, the shadow of the clouds was also removed. But neither did the light remain there, because the measure of its hours was fulfilled, and it sank into the waters that were under the firmament. So, with the firmament, nothing moved upward, because nothing was left over it; it was appointed to divide the waters from the waters, and was not appointed to divide light from darkness.

Thus there was no light in the first night of the world’s making; but in the second and third nights, as we have said, the light sank into the waters that were under the firmament, and from them it emerged (passed through them). In the fourth night, when the waters had been gathered into one place and, as is said, light had been brought to order (created), then from it and from the fire there came forth the sun, the moon, and the stars. And to all these heavenly lights their places were appointed: the moon was set on the west of the firmament, the sun on the east, and the stars in that same hour were scattered and arranged throughout the firmament.

Of the light that was on the first day God said, “it is good”; but of the firmament, created on the second day, He said no such thing, for it was not yet wholly complete, had not received its full ordering and adornment. The Creator delayed pronouncing the word of approval until the heavenly lights came forth, so that when the firmament should be adorned with the sun, the moon, and the stars, and these lights, shining upon the firmament, should scatter the deep darkness on it, then He might pronounce of the firmament too what He had said of the other creatures, namely that they are “very good.”

Having spoken of the firmament produced on the second day, Moses turns to recount the gathering of the waters and the grasses and trees that the earth produced on the third day, and says thus: “And God said: Let the water under the heaven be gathered together unto one place, and let the dry land appear” (Gen 1:9). The saying, “Let the water be gathered into one place,” gives us to understand that the earth supported the waters, and that the depths were not under the earth, hanging upon nothing. So on that same night, as soon as God spoke, the waters were gathered into one, and the surface of the earth was dried in the blink of an eye.

When both these things had been done, God in the morning commanded the earth to bring forth every kind of herb and grass, and also various fruit-bearing trees. The herbs at the time of their creation came forth (appeared) as products of a single instant, but to look at they seemed products of months. So also the trees at the time of their creation appeared as products of a single day, but in completeness and in fruits weighing down their branches, they seemed products of years. The herbs needed for food were prepared for the animals, which were created two days later; the Lord also created the grain (corn-ears) needed for the food of Adam and Eve, whom God expelled from paradise four days later.

Having spoken of the gathering of the waters and of the earth’s growths on the third day, Moses turns to recount the heavenly lights created on the firmament, and says: “And God said: Let there be lights in the firmament of heaven, to divide the day from the night” (Gen 1:14) — that is, let one of them rule the day, and the other rule the night. God said: “and let them be for signs (of the hours), and for seasons (the marking of summer and winter), and for days (the rising and setting of the sun), and for years” (Gen 1:14), because the years are composed of the days of the sun and the months of the moon.

It is said: “God made two great lights: the greater light at the beginning of the day, and the lesser light at the beginning of the night, and the stars” (Gen 1:16). On the days before the fourth, the creation of creatures pertained to the evening, but the ordering of creatures on the fourth day was carried out in the morning. After the third day had ended and it was said, “And the evening and the morning were the third day” (Gen 1:13), God did not create the two lights in the evening, lest the order of night and day be disturbed and the morning come before the evening.

Since the following days too followed the same order as the first day, the night of the fourth day, like the nights before it, preceded its day. If the evening of that day was earlier than its morning, it follows that the lights were created not in the evening but in the morning. That one of the lights was created in the evening and the other in the morning, the following does not permit: “Let there be lights” (Gen 1:14), and “God made two great lights” (Gen 1:16). If the lights at the time of their creation were great and were created in the morning, it follows that the sun stood at that time in the east, and the moon opposite it in the west; the sun seemed low and partly sunken, because it was created at the place of its rising over the earth; and the moon stood higher, because she was created where she is on the fifteenth day. So when the sun became visible over the earth, the two lights saw each other, and then the moon seemed to sink. The very place where the moon was at her creation, her magnitude and brightness, show that she was created in the appearance she has on the fifteenth day.

Just as the trees, herbs, animals, birds, and man appeared both old and young at once — old by the appearance of their limbs and parts, and young by the time of their creation — so the moon also was at once old and young: young, because she had just been created; old, because she was full, as on the fifteenth day. If God had created the moon in the form we see her on the first or second day, then by reason of her nearness to the sun she could not have shone, or even been visible. If the moon had appeared as she is on the fourth day, although she would have been visible, she would not have shone, and untrue would have been the saying, “God made two great lights,” as well as, “Let there be lights in the firmament of heaven, to give light upon the earth” (Gen 1:14). As the moon was created in the appearance we see on the fifteenth day, so the sun, though it was its first day, in its creation appeared as if four days old, because all days are reckoned and were reckoned by the sun. The eleven days by which the moon is “older” than the sun, and which were added to the moon in the first year, are the very days yearly added to the moon by those who use lunar reckoning. The year of Adam was not an incomplete year, because the missing number of the moon’s days was made up at the very moment of her creation. According to this year, Adam’s descendants learned to add eleven days to each year. From this it is clear that it was not the Chaldeans who established this way of reckoning times and years, but it was established already before Adam.

Having spoken of the lights created on the firmament, Moses turns to recount the creeping things, birds, and great whales which were created out of the waters on the fifth day, and says: “And God said: Let the waters bring forth abundantly the moving creature that hath life, and fowl that may fly above the earth... And God created great whales, and every living creature that moveth, which the waters brought forth abundantly, after their kind” (Gen 1:20–21). When, after the gathering of the waters on the second day, the rivers, springs, lakes, and marshes appeared, then the waters, scattered throughout the universe, by the word of God brought forth from themselves creeping things and fish; in the deeps were created whales; and amid the waves at the same time the birds soared into the air. The making of Leviathan (the whale) and Behemoth is mentioned also by the prophets. Of the first they say that he lives in the sea (Ps 64:8); of Behemoth, Job says that he lives on dry land (Job 40:10). And David says of him that he grazes on the mountains (Ps 49:11). It is likely that after their creation places of dwelling were appointed to them, that Leviathan should dwell in the sea and Behemoth on the dry land.

Having spoken of the creation of creeping things, birds, and whales on the fifth day, Moses passes to describe the creation of those creeping things, beasts, and cattle which were created on the sixth day, and says: “And God said: Let the earth bring forth the living creature after his kind, cattle, and creeping thing, and beast of the earth” (Gen 1:24). Creeping things the earth brought forth everywhere; but beasts and cattle were created near paradise, that they might dwell beside Adam. So the earth, at God’s command, immediately brought forth creeping things, beasts of the field, beasts of prey, and cattle, as many as were needed for the service of him who on the same day transgressed the commandment of his Lord!

Having spoken of the creation of creeping things, beasts, and cattle on the sixth day, Moses turns to recount the creation of man, who was created on the same day, and says: “And God said.” To whom does God speak here and elsewhere during creation? Clearly, He speaks to His Son. Of the Son the Evangelist said, “All things were made by him; and without him was not anything made that was made” (John 1:3). Paul also points to Him, saying, “By him were all things created, that are in heaven, and that are in earth, visible and invisible” (Col 1:16).

“And God said: Let us make man in our image” (Gen 1:26) — that is, let him have authority, if he wills, to obey Us. Why are we the image of God? Moses explains this with the following words: “let them have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air, and over the cattle, and over all the earth” (Gen 1:26). Therefore the dominion which man received over the earth and over all that is on it is the image of God, who possesses the things above and the things below.

By the words, “male and female created he them” (Gen 1:27), Moses gives us to understand that Eve was already in Adam, in that rib which was taken from Adam. Although Eve was in him not in mind, but in body, yet not in body only, but also in soul and spirit, for God added nothing to the rib taken from Adam except beauty and outward form. Since in the rib itself there was everything needed for the forming of Eve from it, it is justly said, “male and female created he them.”

“And God blessed them, and God said unto them: Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth, and subdue it: and have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air, and over every living thing that moveth upon the earth” (Gen 1:28). God blessed our first parents on the earth, because even before they had sinned He was preparing the earth as their dwelling place — for before they sinned, God knew that they would sin.

“Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish” — it does not say paradise, but “the earth… and have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air, and over all the cattle.” But how could the first parents have dominion over the fish of the sea, when no sea was nearby? How could they have dominion over the birds that flew throughout all the ends of the universe, unless the offspring of the first parents was afterward to settle the ends of the universe? And how could they have dominion over all the beasts of the earth, unless their race was afterward to dwell over all the earth?

Although Adam was created and received a blessing to have dominion over the earth and over all that is on it, yet God set him in paradise. Thus God, in pronouncing a blessing upon our first parents, showed His foreknowledge; and by setting him in paradise, He revealed His goodness. That none might say, “Paradise was not created for man,” God set him in paradise; and that none might say, “God did not know that man would sin,” He blessed man upon the earth. Moreover, God blessed man before man’s transgression of the commandment, so that the transgression of the one who received the blessing should not withhold the blessing of the One who blesses; that the world should not be returned to nothingness because of the senselessness of him for whose sake all was created. God blessed man not in paradise, for paradise and all that is in it are blessed; He blessed before settling him in paradise, on the earth, that by a blessing which goodness anticipated, the force of the curse which by God’s righteousness soon smote the earth might be lessened. The blessing was only in promise, for it was fulfilled only after man was expelled from paradise. But grace appeared in actual fact, for on the same day it settled man in paradise, adorned him with glory, and delivered into his power all the trees of paradise.
